nativeRange.habitat: Outer reef slopes and drop-offs; forms large aggregations along upper drop-off edges at depths of 2–40 m

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and overhangs

diet.notes: Feeds primarily on zooplankton, planktonic crustaceans, and fish eggs in the wild. In captivity, generally advised to offer a variety of frozen and live foods including mysid shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, and other zooplankton substitutes. Frequent small feedings (3–5 times daily) have been recommended to maintain body condition.

avoidWith: other male anthias of the same species

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe, 2366x1775. Tack-sharp side profile of a male in full nuptial colour: violet body, red dorsal fin, orange/yellow face and snout, blue tail on a natural reef. Best diagnostic + most striking.
Rickard Zerpe, 2507x1880. Sharp side profile showing the bold orange-yellow lateral stripe over a magenta/pink body (the yellowstriped fairy basslet look); different individual/colour emphasis.

Jenny (JennyHuang), 1542x1077. Sharp full-body shot of a pinker/female-form individual with red dorsal edge, yellow face and blue tail; different photographer, adds colour-form variety for the haremic group.

summary: The Queen Purple Anthias is a deep-pink-to-violet reef fish from Indo-Pacific outer reef slopes, generally considered expert-level due to demanding feeding requirements and high stress sensitivity. Best kept in a small group with one male and several females.
careNotes:
The Queen Purple Anthias tends to be among the more challenging anthias species to maintain long-term in captivity. It is consistently rated as expert-only, with a pronounced tendency to refuse prepared foods and a notable susceptibility to bacterial infections and parasites when stressed or underfed. Frequent small feedings (3–5 times daily) are generally advised, using a variety of live and frozen zooplankton-type foods. A well-established, stable system with excellent water quality has been reported to improve long-term survival odds.

This species is a protogynous hermaphrodite, meaning all individuals are born female and a dominant individual will transition to male over time. Haremic social groups — one male with several females — are the natural social structure and are recommended in captivity. A single specimen is generally considered less viable long-term than a small group. Males are distinguishable by a pointed snout, a dark blotch on the rear dorsal fin, extended caudal fin lobes, and taller second dorsal and anal fin rays compared to females.
breedingNotes: The Queen Purple Anthias is a protogynous hermaphrodite — all individuals are born female, and a dominant fish will eventually transition to male. Captive breeding is considered extremely difficult. Spawning in large, well-maintained systems has been reported, but rearing larvae through to settlement has not been reliably documented in home aquaria.

additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (12 cm TL), temperature range (22–28°C vs LiveAquaria 22–25.6°C — averaged to 22–27°C), and confirmed family Anthiadidae as current taxonomy. Reef2Reef wiki returned HTTP 403.

fish.breedingNotes: The Queen Purple Anthias is a protogynous hermaphrodite; all individuals begin as females, with a dominant fish transitioning to male over time. Spawning in large established systems has been reported, but reliable larval rearing through to settlement is not documented for home aquaria.
